questions: Should the auto-clamp (Step 4) fire silently, or should there be some brief visual feedback when a clip's duration is corrected after async metadata arrives? The clip will visually shrink, which could be disorienting.

For 'Hold last frame' (Step 8): should this create a single clip with a holdTail property, or split into a video clip + a hold clip? Single-clip is cleaner for the data model but needs renderer changes; two-clip reuses existing hold infrastructure but complicates shot group management.

When a user manually resizes a clip past its media end (creating intentional overhang), should the auto-clamp (Step 4) respect that and leave it alone? If so, we need a way to distinguish 'accidental overhang from bad duration data' vs 'user deliberately extended the clip.'
Are there any clips in production data that intentionally have to > assetDuration today? If so, the auto-clamp could break existing projects. We'd need a migration or opt-in approach.

success_criteria.criterion: No clip created via shot drop or switch-to-final-video has to > assetDuration when assetDuration is known at creation time
Clips created with unknown duration are auto-corrected when the real duration becomes available
Timeline visually distinguishes the overhang zone (dead time) from playable media with a stripe/overlay pattern
Right-click context menu on an overhang clip offers 'Trim to media end' that correctly shortens the clip
Right-click context menu on an overhang clip offers 'Hold last frame' that converts dead time to intentional hold
Existing clips without overhang are completely unaffected — no visual changes, no duration changes
All existing tests continue to pass (updated where necessary for new clamping behavior)
Unit tests cover overhang detection, clamping, and hold conversion utilities
Overhang indicator is consistent with existing design language (amber tones matching resize clamp indicator)
Auto-clamp does not fire for hold clips or image clips (only video clips with from/to exceeding asset duration)
The overhang zone shows a tooltip on hover explaining the dead time and available actions
Manual smoke tests pass: drop with known duration, drop with unknown duration + late resolve, switch-to-final-video, resize past media end, trim action, hold action
New overhang.ts utility is under 100 lines with no dependencies beyond types

assumptions: The primary cause of dead time is the 5-second fallback when video duration is unknown at drop time, combined with no correction when the real duration is later discovered.
The `hold` clip type and property already work correctly for intentional freeze frames — we can build on this for the 'hold last frame' feature.
Resize on visual clips adjusts speed rather than extending to, so resize is not a primary source of overhang (confirmed from useClipResize.ts).
The existing context menu in ClipAction is the right place for trim/hold actions (no need for a new UI surface).
Auto-clamping when asset duration resolves is acceptable UX — users would prefer their clips to be correct rather than preserving a wrong duration.
The diagonal-stripe overhang indicator pattern is visually distinct enough without being too alarming — consistent with the amber-400 resize clamp indicator already in use.
